Name: rate_distortion_compression_framework
Title: A Unified Rate-Distortion Framework for Efficient Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: Can we derive a comprehensive framework that optimally allocates bits in model compression while preserving predictive sufficiency, using a rate-distortion perspective? This framework will challenge existing empirical heuristics by grounding them in quantifiable information-theoretic limits, making explicit connections between model architecture, task complexity, and data types.
Related Work: Current literature addresses various aspects of rate-distortion theory but often lacks a unified approach that systematically links model architecture and task complexity to bit allocation in compression. Notable works, such as 'Radio: Rate-Distortion Optimization for Large Language Model Compression,' provide insights but do not integrate these elements into a cohesive framework. This proposal aims to fill that gap, offering a novel perspective on compression informed by a solid theoretical foundation.
Abstract: In this proposal, we introduce a unified rate-distortion framework for model compression that seeks to optimize bit allocation while maintaining predictive sufficiency. By framing model compression as a lossy source coding problem under a distortion constraint, we will derive achievable rates and distortion-rate curves that explicitly consider the interaction between model architecture, task complexity, and data characteristics. This framework will leverage measures of task-relevant information, such as mutual information and entropy, to guide the compression process. We will demonstrate how this approach can yield significant improvements over existing empirical methods by providing a clear operational meaning and establishing theoretical limits on compression efficiency. Experimental validation will involve applying our framework to various model types and tasks, assessing the trade-offs between model size and predictive accuracy against established benchmarks in the literature.
Experiments: 1. Develop a theoretical model based on rate-distortion principles and derive achievable rates for various model architectures. 2. Implement a systematic method for bit allocation in model compression using mutual information as a guiding metric. 3. Validate the framework through empirical experiments on standard datasets, comparing the predictive performance of compressed models against baseline models using metrics such as accuracy, F1-score, and bit-per-weight ratios. 4. Analyze the impact of task complexity and data characteristics on compression efficiency, providing a detailed examination of trade-offs.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential limitations include the challenge of accurately estimating mutual information and entropy, especially in high-dimensional spaces. Additionally, the complexity of the theoretical framework may pose difficulties in practical implementation and validation. The effectiveness of the proposed method will also depend on the selection of appropriate datasets and tasks that are representative of real-world applications.
